note: These tasks were from the reify project but ended up in dark-factory's tasks.json via shared Taskmaster MCP. IDs are from dark-factory's numbering and will need remapping.

tasks.title: Fix tree-sitter-reify grammar: line comments (//) and block comments (/* */)
tasks.description: The tree-sitter-reify grammar uses '#' for line comments, but the Reify language spec mandates '//'. Block comments '/* */' are also entirely absent. High-priority spec compliance bug: all .ri source files using spec-correct '//' comments will fail to parse.
tasks.details:
## Problem
- Current grammar: `# comment` (Python-style hash)
- Spec-required: `// comment` (C-style double-slash)
- Missing entirely: `/* block comment */`

## Impact
All .ri source files using spec-correct comment syntax fail to parse — affects full pipeline: tree-sitter → reify-syntax → reify-compiler → LSP.

## Fix Steps
1. In `tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js`, locate the comment rules
2. Change line comment token from hash-based regex to `\/\/[^\n]*/`
3. Add block comment rule: `seq('/*', /[^*]*\*+([^/*][^*]*\*+)*/, '/')`
4. Run `tree-sitter generate` to regenerate `src/` files
5. Update any .ri test fixtures using `#` comments → `//`
6. Verify all parser tests pass

## memory_hints
- search: "tree-sitter grammar line comments spec compliance reify"
- search: "block comments missing grammar reify"
- mem0: 98019364 (grammar uses #), e2d13a5f (spec uses //), 20a732e9 (/* */ missing)

tasks.title: Add minimize/maximize declaration grammar rules to tree-sitter-reify
tasks.description: The tree-sitter-reify grammar has no rules for minimize/maximize optimization objective declarations, despite these being required for M3 optimization objectives and confirmed in the Reify language spec. This gap persisted through the M5 language breadth run.
tasks.details:
## Problem
The Reify language spec defines `minimize` and `maximize` as valid top-level optimization objective declarations (M3 Auto Resolution). The tree-sitter-reify grammar has no rules covering these constructs, meaning:
- Optimizer objectives in .ri files cannot be parsed
- The constraint solver's objective pathway (via argmin/Nelder-Mead) has no syntactic entry point

## Context
- Task #24 (NLopt/argmin solver) was marked done, but the objective support via argmin cannot be reached from .ri source through parse→compile pipeline
- This is a related gap — fixing the grammar is the first step toward end-to-end objective solving

## Fix Steps
1. Review Reify language spec (docs/reify-language-spec.md section on Constraint System / optimization)
2. Add `minimize_decl` and `maximize_decl` grammar rules to `tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js`
3. Run `tree-sitter generate` to regenerate parser
4. Add parser tests with sample minimize/maximize declarations
5. Wire the new AST nodes into reify-syntax lowering pass

## memory_hints
- search: "minimize maximize declarations grammar tree-sitter M3"
- search: "reify-constraints NLopt argmin solver decision rationale"
- mem0: 0f36926b (gap observation), 785dcd7e (argmin solver only)
- graphiti: c18c888d (minimize method), 7fda5a48 (maximize method)

tasks.title: Fix apply_lambda: pass populated EvalContext (function registry) so lambdas can call user-defined functions
tasks.description: apply_lambda() constructs an EvalContext via EvalContext::simple with an empty function registry. Any lambda that calls a user-defined function via .map/.filter/.fold silently returns Value::Undef instead of the correct result. This is a correctness bug — user-defined function calls inside lambdas are effectively no-ops.
tasks.details:
## Bug Description
apply_lambda() uses EvalContext::simple (empty function registry). When the lambda body contains a call to a user-defined function (e.g. via .map/.filter/.fold), the EvalContext has no registered functions, so the lookup fails and the call silently returns Value::Undef.

## Impact
All higher-order usage of user-defined functions via lambdas is broken:
- `my_list.map(|x| my_fn(x))` → always returns [Undef, Undef, ...]
- `my_list.filter(|x| predicate(x))` → always empty
- `my_list.fold(init, |acc, x| combine(acc, x))` → always Undef

## Fix
In apply_lambda(), propagate the caller's EvalContext (or at minimum pass the function registry) instead of constructing a fresh empty one via EvalContext::simple.

## memory_hints
- search: "apply_lambda EvalContext function registry lambda user-defined function undef"
- search: "EvalContext simple empty registry bug lambda map filter fold"
- entity: "apply_lambda"
- mem0: 6237ba6e, ae6449b2, 7da6810f

tasks.title: Fix TopologyTemplate: preserve trait_bounds info through lowering pass
tasks.description: TopologyTemplate loses trait_bounds information during its lowering/compilation pass. This was flagged as a correctness gap during the task 67 (trait bounds) review. Trait bound constraints attached to topology template parameters are silently dropped, leading to incomplete type checking at instantiation sites.
tasks.details:
## Problem
During the task 67 review (trait bounds implementation), TopologyTemplate was identified as not preserving trait_bounds through the lowering pass. This means:
- Topology templates with parameterized types that have trait constraints lose those constraints
- Instantiation of topology templates does not enforce trait bounds on type arguments
- Silent correctness gap — no compile error where one should occur

## Fix Steps
1. Audit the TopologyTemplate lowering in reify-compiler for trait_bounds fields
2. Ensure trait_bounds info is stored in the TopologyTemplate AST/IR node
3. Propagate to instantiation-time checking
4. Add test: topology template with trait-bounded type param, verify violation is caught

## memory_hints
- search: "TopologyTemplate trait_bounds info loss lowering task 67"
- search: "topology template trait bounds type parameter constraint checking"
- entity: "TopologyTemplate"

tasks.title: Add 3-hop trait bound chain integration test (deferred from task 67 review)
tasks.description: No integration test covers a 3-hop trait bound chain (A: T1, T1: T2, T2: T3). Add an end-to-end test to verify multi-hop bound satisfaction is correctly resolved. Deferred from the task 67 (trait bounds) robustness review.
tasks.details:
## Background
Task 67 review flagged that multi-hop trait bound chains are not tested end-to-end. A 3-hop chain exercises the full transitive bound resolution algorithm.

## Fix Steps
1. Write a .ri fixture that defines:
   - Trait T3 with method m3
   - Trait T2: T3
   - Trait T1: T2
   - Structure A implementing T1 (and thus transitively T2, T3)
2. Add parser + compiler integration test verifying A satisfies T1, T2, and T3
3. Add negative test: structure missing T3 implementation should fail

tasks.title: Skip non-generic trait bound checks earlier in the pipeline (deferred from task 67 review)
tasks.description: Non-generic structures currently go through the full trait bound checking pipeline unnecessarily. Add an early-exit for structures with no type parameters. Deferred from the task 67 (trait bounds) robustness review.
tasks.details:
## Background
Task 67 review flagged that non-generic structures (those without type parameters) are processed through the trait bound resolution pipeline even though they have nothing to check. Adding an early-exit guard would improve performance and clarity.

## Fix Steps
1. In the trait bound checking pass, identify the entry point for per-structure checking
2. Add guard: if structure has no type parameters, skip trait bound resolution
3. Add test verifying non-generic structures produce no bound check overhead
4. Benchmark improvement (optional)

tasks.title: Consolidate pre-passes in reify-compiler into a single ordered pipeline (deferred from task 67 review)
tasks.description: The reify-compiler has multiple pre-passes that run before main compilation. These are not organized as a single declared pipeline, making ordering implicit and fragile. Consolidate into an explicit ordered pre-pass pipeline. Deferred from the task 67 (trait bounds) robustness review.
tasks.details:
## Background
Task 67 review identified that pre-passes (pre-compilation transformations) are scattered and their ordering is implicit in the call graph. Consolidating into a declared pipeline would clarify dependencies and make it easier to add/reorder passes.

## Fix Steps
1. Audit all pre-passes in reify-compiler (e.g. trait collection, type resolution, bound checking setup)
2. Define a `PrePassPipeline` structure with explicitly ordered passes
3. Migrate existing pre-pass invocations into the pipeline
4. Add test verifying all pre-passes run in correct order

## memory_hints
- search: "consolidate pre-passes compiler pipeline task 67 review"

tasks.title: Fix is_dirty() mutex race in concurrent_eval.rs:176
tasks.description: The is_dirty() check in concurrent_eval.rs at line 176 runs outside the Mutex critical section for skip_state, creating a TOCTOU race. With 3+ concurrent parents, skip decisions can be inconsistent. No fix has been implemented yet.
tasks.details:
## Problem
`is_dirty()` is called outside the Mutex guard for `skip_state` in `concurrent_eval.rs` line 176. This creates a time-of-check/time-of-use race condition in the concurrent scheduler. With 3 or more concurrent parent nodes, skip decisions can become inconsistent.

## Fix approach
Move `is_dirty()` inside the Mutex critical section, or use an atomic flag instead of a mutex-guarded bool for the dirty check.

## memory_hints
- search: "is_dirty skip_state mutex concurrent scheduler race"
- search: "concurrent_eval skip state race condition concurrent parents"
- entity: "dirty/demand cone computation"

tasks.title: Add minimize/maximize grammar rules to tree-sitter-reify
tasks.description: tree-sitter-reify has no grammar rules for minimize/maximize declarations, despite these being required for M3 optimization objectives. This gap blocks parsing of optimization objective syntax from .ri source files.
tasks.details:
## Problem
The grammar.js in tree-sitter-reify does not define rules for `minimize` and `maximize` declarations. These are required for M3 optimization objective support. Without grammar rules, the parser cannot recognize these constructs from .ri source.

## Known context
- Memory ID: 0f36926b-6bf7-40cc-bd82-a277ab11c3a0
- Graphiti edge: eb015025-11d8-4f72-807a-c55fb1dff1c1
- Previous reconciliation created Task #55 for this; task tree was subsequently cleared
- Companion gap: argmin objective also unreachable from .ri source (separate task)

## Steps
1. Define `minimize_decl` and `maximize_decl` grammar rules in grammar.js
2. Add to top-level statement rules
3. Run `npx tree-sitter generate` to regenerate parser.c
4. Add parser tests for minimize/maximize syntax

## memory_hints
- search: "minimize maximize grammar tree-sitter optimization objective"
- search: "tree-sitter-reify grammar rules M3 optimization"
- entity: "tree-sitter-reify"

tasks.title: Wire argmin optimization objective into parse→compile pipeline
tasks.description: Task #24 was closed with argmin implemented in the optimizer, but the argmin optimization objective cannot be reached from .ri source via the parse→compile pipeline. The pipeline gap must be closed end-to-end.
tasks.details:
## Problem
argmin is implemented at the optimizer/IR level, but is unreachable from .ri source code through the parse→compile pipeline. Task #24 was prematurely closed.

## Evidence
- Graphiti edge f7462a9e-38f2-43d7-876a-725f46bc59f2: argmin cannot be reached from .ri source code
- parse leads to compile (graphiti edge 7e8bcd14)
- .ri source code is part of the parse process

## Investigation needed
1. Identify where the pipeline breaks (parser? AST lowering? IR emission?)
2. Check whether grammar rules for argmin exist in tree-sitter-reify
3. Add AST→IR lowering for argmin objective declarations
4. Write integration test: .ri file with argmin → solver invocation

tasks.title: Implement reify-slvs-sys crate (hand-written FFI to system libslvs1-dev)
tasks.description: Decision finalized 2026-03-20: use system libslvs1-dev + hand-written FFI in a reify-slvs-sys crate (Option D). The slvs crate v0.6.0 does not compile with Rust 1.94.0, making this the chosen path forward.
tasks.details:
## Context
- Decision finalized 2026-03-20 (graphiti edges: a1df7bed, 8798bd5a)
- The slvs crate (SolveSpace Rust bindings, v0.6.0) does not compile with Rust 1.94.0
- Decided on Option D: system library + hand-written FFI crate
- SolveSpace C API (slvs.h) is simple: ~10 structs and 1 function Slvs_Solve()
- reify uses a Rust wrapper and stateless Slvs_Solve

## Steps
1. Create `reify-slvs-sys` crate in workspace
2. Write FFI bindings targeting `libslvs1-dev` system package
3. Bind the ~10 structs and `Slvs_Solve()` from slvs.h
4. Write a thin safe wrapper layer for use by reify-constraints
5. Add build.rs linking against system libslvs1
6. Test against existing SolveSpace integration tests

## memory_hints
- search: "SolveSpace libslvs reify-slvs-sys FFI Option D"
- search: "slvs crate Rust 1.94 compile failure reify"
- search: "Slvs_Solve stateless API reify constraints solver"
